fr:mainmatter.html:p: A (left) strength of a monad T with respect to a monoidal structure \otimes is a natural transformation \sigma : \Gamma \otimes T A \to T (\Gamma \otimes A) satisfying certain laws. A concise formulation is that a left-strength is a lifting of T from a monad on categories to a monad on actegories, with the monoidal category \mathcal C acting on itself. If \mathcal C is a monoidal closed category, then strength is equivalently an enrichment of T.

The most general version of this is given by Slattery: a strong monad on multicategories is given by a unit map \mathcal C(X;T X) and an extension map \mathcal C(\Gamma ,X,\Delta ;TY) \to \mathcal C(\Gamma ,TX,\Delta ;TY) satisfying some laws. This makes the connection to the semantics of effectful programming languages apparent: a monad only has an extension map for unary morphisms but a strength allows for an extension of any single input of a multimorphism. In programming languages terms, the strength is what allows for the use of a monadic bind in the presence of other variables.
Despite the common term strong monad, a strength is in general a structure, though in some special cases it is a property.
All monads on the category of sets are canonically Set-enriched and therefore carry a strength. Non-trivial strengths abound in (pre)sheaf semantics.

fr:title.text: Example of a monad with no strength: Allocation on Indexed Sets

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Example of a monad with no strength: Allocation on Indexed Sets

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Consider the following monad on \textrm {Set}^\mathbb {N}:
\begin {align*} T(X) n &= \sum _{m : \mathbb {N}} X (n + m)\\ \eta n x &= (0, x) \\ (f : X \to TY)^\dagger n (m , x) &= (m + p , y) && \textrm {where } f (n + m) x = (p , y) \end {align*} Our intuition is that \textrm {Set}^\mathbb {N} is a simple (but deficient) model of allocation: the \mathbb {N} represents the number of resources that have been allocated, so that the meaning of types can be dependent on this. Then this monad is an attempt at an allocation monad: an element tells you how many new cells are allocated and gives you a value valid in that future. But this monad does not work for typical denotational semantics because the monad cannot be given a strength with respect to the cartesian product monoidal structure.

Aside: The easiest way to verify that this is a monad is to see that the construction is left adjoint to the forgetful U : \textrm {Set}^{\omega ^\textrm {op}} \to \textrm {Set}^\mathbb {N} and this is the induced monad. In fact this adjunction is monadic.

A strength would be a natural \sigma : \Gamma \times T(X) \to T(\Gamma \times X). Intuitively such a morphism would send (\gamma , (m , x)) to (m , (\uparrow \gamma , x)) but the problem is that no such \uparrow \gamma is guaranteed to exist, and therefore a strength cannot be constructed.

As a concrete counterexample, pick \Gamma = y(0) and A = y(1) where y(m) n = m \equiv n is the Yoneda embedding/Dirac \delta . Note that T 0 \cong 0 and y(0) \times y(1) \cong 0. But the strength gives us a morphism y(0)\times T(y(1)) \to T(y(0)\times y(1)) \cong T0\cong 0. Since \textrm {Set}^\mathbb {N} is cartesian closed, 0 is strict initial so this implies that y(0)\times T(y(1)) \cong 0 but this is false as there is an element (\textrm {refl}(0), (1, \textrm {refl}(1))) : (y(0) \times T(y(1))) 0.

What is the "algebraic" structure that this monad captures? Well as above it is equivalent to a presheaf structure \textrm {Set}^{\omega ^\textrm {op}}. This can be equivalently stated as a family of maps \uparrow _n : X (n+1) \to X n for every n : \mathbb {N}. This can be expressed as a morphism (y1 \multimap X) \to X in \textrm {Set}^\mathbb {N}. So this also provides an example of an algebra with respect to "linear" arities that is not a strong monad. This is why Staton reformulates the notion of a block-algebra, which Plotkin and Power had stated as a similar linear operation.

fr:title.text: Pseudocommutativity and lax idempotency for relative pseudomonads

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Pseudocommutativity and lax idempotency for relative pseudomonads

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Includes, among other things, a definition of strong (relative) monad on a multicategories.
